_clinical_notes: Grade I: mild, can walk, minimal pain on palpation. Grade II: moderate, limp, palpable tenderness, pain with resisted knee flexion. Grade III: complete rupture or proximal avulsion — palpable defect, cannot flex knee against gravity, sudden proximal pain in young athletes = avulsion risk. Proximal avulsion (near ischial tuberosity) especially in adolescents requires imaging.

mechanism.prompt: How did the hamstring get injured?

options.label: Direct blow to the back of the thigh

direct_impact.prompt: After the blow, can you walk normally?

location.prompt: Where is most of the pain in your leg?

options.label: High up near the sit bone / buttock crease
Middle of the back of the thigh
Back of the knee / lower thigh

proximal_check.prompt: Is the pain right at the sit bone (the bony bump you sit on), and does it feel sharp or tearing rather than just sore?

options.label: Yes, sharp pain right at the sit bone
Sore in the upper hamstring but not at the bone

pop_hs.prompt: Did you feel a pop, snap, or sudden giving-way sensation?

options.label: Yes, a clear pop or snap

function_hs.prompt: How is your walking ability right now?

knee_flex.prompt: Can you bend your knee and try to lift your heel toward your buttock? (gentle attempt only — don't force it)

options.label: Too painful to attempt or feels blocked

outcome.seek_care_reason: Pain at the sit bone after a sudden hamstring injury can indicate a proximal avulsion — a tearing of the tendon from the bone. This needs imaging to rule out.
Your symptoms suggest a severe (Grade III) hamstring strain or possible muscle rupture. You should be evaluated by a medical professional.
